title: Motion in Two Dimensions (Vectors & Projectile Motion)
content:
Motion in Two Dimensions

In this lecture, we extend our study of kinematics to two dimensions. Many real-world motions, such as throwing a ball or a car turning a corner, involve movement in more than one direction at once.

Vectors in Kinematics

In two dimensions, position, velocity, and acceleration are all vector quantities. A vector has both magnitude and direction. We use arrows to represent vectors graphically and components to represent them mathematically.

  • Position Vector: $\vec{r} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j}$
  • Displacement Vector: $\Delta\vec{r} = \vec{r}_f - \vec{r}_i$
  • Velocity Vector: $\vec{v} = rac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$
  • Acceleration Vector: $\vec{a} = rac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$

Projectile Motion

Projectile motion is a classic example of two-dimensional motion. A projectile is any object thrown or launched into the air, subject only to gravity (ignoring air resistance).

  • The horizontal and vertical motions are independent of each other.
  • Horizontal motion: Constant velocity ($a_x = 0$)
  • Vertical motion: Constant acceleration ($a_y = -g$)

The equations for projectile motion are:

  • $x = v_{0x} t$
  • $y = v_{0y} t - rac{1}{2}gt^2$

Where $v_{0x}$ and $v_{0y}$ are the initial velocity components.

Example Problem

A ball is launched with a speed of 20 m/s at an angle of 30° above the horizontal. Find the time of flight, maximum height, and range.

  • Resolve velocity: $v_{0x} = 20 \cos 30^\circ$, $v_{0y} = 20 \sin 30^\circ$
  • Time of flight: $t = rac{2v_{0y}}{g}$
  • Maximum height: $h = rac{v_{0y}^2}{2g}$
  • Range: $R = v_{0x} \cdot t$
